Reduced Inequalities.reason: Game theory, especially "Bargaining," provides essential models for analyzing policies on income distribution and social protection aimed at achieving greater equality (Target 10.4).

Climate Action.reason: Topics like "Repeated games: the prisoner dilemma" and "Bargaining" are key tools for modeling international climate negotiations and policy integration (Target 13.2).

Life on Land.reason: The course's game theory models ("Repeated games," "Bargaining") are applicable to analyzing the sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems and common resources (Target 15.1).

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: Game theory is fundamental to modeling conflict, cooperation, and developing effective institutions (Target 16.6). "Bargaining" and "Repeated games" are directly applicable.

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course analyzes strategic interactions through "Bargaining" and "Repeated games," which are foundational to forming effective partnerships and enhancing policy coherence (Targets 17.14, 17.16).
